subject: general
title: Parents don't send children regularly — chronic absenteeism
query_text: Parents don't send children regularly — chronic absenteeism

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Start a simple attendance chart on the blackboard — let students mark their own presence with a tally. This builds ownership and peer awareness.
Identify 3–5 chronically absent students. Speak privately with a trusted peer from their neighbourhood — ask them to walk to school together.
Send a short, friendly note home (written in local language) celebrating the child's contribution in class — not about absence, but about belonging.

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — 'My School Story' Wall
classroom_activity.activity: Give each student a small piece of paper. Ask them to draw or write one thing they enjoy about school. Pin all responses on a 'Our Class Wall' section of the blackboard. When absent students return, show them — 'We missed your story here.' This builds emotional connection to school.
classroom_activity.materials: Scrap paper, chalk, blackboard — nothing extra needed

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: For students who miss lessons frequently, assign a 'buddy' who shares notes and catches them up in 5 minutes before class starts. This reduces the fear of falling behind — which itself is a hidden reason children stop coming.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After trying the buddy system for two weeks, which students showed improved attendance — and what did their buddies do differently that seemed to help?

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: NEP 2020, Section 6.4–6.5 — Equitable and Inclusive Education: peer tutoring, counsellor/social worker engagement, and community-based strategies for improving attendance among SEDGs
